title: Registration + Coffee & Tea at SRCCON
description: Get your badges and gear up for the first day of SRCCON! (Please grab breakfast on your own before you join us, but we'll also put out some awesome morning snacks a little later into our conference day.)

title: Welcome to SRCCON!
description: We'll kick things off with a welcome, some scene-setting, and some suggestions for getting the most out of SRCCON.

title: Reporting solutions when the world is on fire
description:
By most accounts, the media’s current approach is neither creating a well-informed public nor truly holding the powerful to account. The industry’s relentless focus on crisis and spectacle has driven away news consumers, fueled deep distrust in institutions and the media itself, and left many journalists burned out or leaving the profession altogether. Audiences are left overwhelmed, disengaged and without the information they need to address the biggest problems facing their communities. 

This workshop will create space for journalists to reflect on how to report on solutions at a time when many feel overwhelmed by the state of the world.  We’ll explore how to cover responses without minimizing harm or injustice, and how to build trust with communities by working in partnership with them. The goal is for participants to leave with actionable ideas, practical approaches and a renewed sense of possibility for reporting constructively toward a better future.

title: Social break
description: Take a break! Grab a snack, catch up with people you haven't seen in a while, say hi to someone new!

title: Beyond the Hype: Practical AI applications for small and medium newsrooms
description:
Artificial Intelligence is dominating industry conversations, but many resource-strapped newsrooms struggle to separate marketing hype from genuine utility. This hands-on session focuses strictly on practical, ethical, and cost-effective AI implementations that add immediate value to editorial workflows and audience engagement strategies.

We will move past theoretical debates to examine live case studies of small news organizations utilizing automation for translation, audio transcription management, and structuring unstructured public datasets. Participants will work through a framework to audit their own newsroom workflows, identify friction points ripe for technical optimization, and establish guardrails to maintain strict editorial integrity. You’ll walk away with a blueprint for small-scale experimentation that doesn't require a dedicated team of engineers.

title: Lunch
description: Lunch is on us! We'll have options for vegetarian, vegan, and gluten-free diets ready to go.

title: Metrics that Matter: Measuring community health over clicks
description:
If our journalism aims to serve communities rather than exploit attention, our analytics must reflect that shift. Standard digital metrics—pageviews, time-on-site, bounce rates—frequently incentivize sensationalism over substance and fail to capture true civic impact. This session is a collaborative working group designed to redefine how newsrooms track success.

We will map out alternative evaluation frameworks based on trust, responsiveness, and sustained community participation. Bringing real-world examples from listening-post models and collaborative reporting networks, we will explore how to build internal dashboards that reward deep community relationship-building. Bring your current analytics headaches; we will brainstorm actionable ways to communicate qualitative community impact to stakeholders, board members, and funders.

title: Social break
description: Take a quick afternoon break to stretch your legs, grab some tea or coffee, and chat with fellow attendees.

title: Emergency Mode: Sustaining newsroom resilience during long-term crises
description:
When climate emergencies, political instability, or infrastructure collapses hit a community, local newsrooms step into high gear. But what happens when 'temporary' crisis mode stretches into weeks, months, or years? The risk of organizational collapse, information breakdown, and profound staff burnout becomes an operational reality.

This session offers an active, structural space to build disaster-preparedness blueprints tailored for news management and digital operations. We will analyze strategies for decentralized editing workflows, backup communication arrays, and cross-newsroom mutual aid networks. Crucially, we will tackle the human element: establishing institutional guardrails that protect the psychological and physical safety of reporters on the front lines of community trauma.

title: SRCCON Day 1 Wrap-up
description: Let's gather briefly to reflect on our first day, share announcements, and look ahead to tomorrow's schedule.

title: Registration + Coffee & Tea at SRCCON
description: Welcome back! Grab some coffee or tea, pick up your badge if you're just arriving, and get ready for Day 2.

title: Welcome to Day 2!
description: Opening remarks, logistics for the day, and a quick community check-in before heading into sessions.

title: Beyond the Alt-Text: Designing truly accessible data journalism
description:
Data journalism frequently relies on highly visual mediums—complex charts, dense interactive maps, and sprawling graphics. Unfortunately, these elements often leave blind, low-vision, or neurodivergent audiences completely excluded from critical public interest reporting. Writing basic image alt-text is a start, but true accessibility requires reimagining data representation from the ground up.

In this technical workshop, we will audit common visualization patterns and rewrite them for screen readers, keyboard navigation, and sonification frameworks. We will discuss building accessible data tables as structural foundations, choosing highly legible color palettes, and creating descriptive text summaries that accurately convey core data insights. Bring an existing data project or graphic concept; we will work together to make it accessible to everyone.

title: Social break
description: Take a breather, grab a snack, and chat with fellow attendees between sessions.

title: Protecting the Perimeter: Digital security workflows for source protection
description:
As surveillance capabilities expand and legal environments grow increasingly hostile toward press freedom, secure communication isn't just a technical preference—it is a vital pillar of investigative journalism safety. This session focuses entirely on the practical operational security (OpSec) protocols required to discover, vet, and protect highly vulnerable sources.

We will move past theoretical risks to demonstrate live, defensive technical pipelines: from initial contact via encrypted channels to sandboxing leaked files and stripping sensitive metadata. Participants will learn how to train non-technical sources on basic digital hygiene without causing unnecessary friction, ensuring that security steps remain simple enough to actually follow under high pressure.

title: Lunch
description: Recharge with lunch! Dietary restrictions will be labeled clearly at our serving stations.

title: Radical Transparency: Open-sourcing your newsroom's business model
description:
Building sustainable community trust requires pulling back the curtain on how news organizations are funded, managed, and structured financially. When audiences understand exactly where money comes from—and precisely where it goes—institutional skepticism begins to dissolve, paving the way for deeper reader-supported membership models.

This strategic session profiles independent newsrooms that have completely open-sourced their operational budgets, salary tiers, and grant funding strings. We will dissect the administrative tools, data templates, and public communication strategies needed to publish clear financial health disclosures. We will also candidly navigate the internal friction points, vulnerabilities, and legal questions that arise when a leadership team chooses to build
